div1.entryFree:
Dig (impf. and partic. digged) 1) to turn up the earth with a spade or other instrument: you must d. with mattock and with spade, Tit. IV, 3, 11. Hml. V, 1, 42. Cymb. IV, 2, 389. Transitively, to denote the result: the adversary is digt himself four yard under the countermines, H5 III, 2, 66 (Fluellen's speech).
2) to form by digging, to excavate: his snout --s sepulchres, Ven. 622. deep trenches, Sonn. 2, 2. R2 III, 3, 169. H4B IV, 5, 111. H6B IV, 10, 55. V, 1, 169. H6C V, 2, 21. Tit. II, 3, 270. Hml. V, 1, 141. With up: if I --ed up thy forefathers' graves, H6C I, 3, 27. Rom. V, 3, 6.
3) to gain by turning up the earth: will d. thee pig-nuts, Tp. II, 2, 172. H4A I, 3, 60. H6A I, 4, 45. Mcb. IV, 1, 25. With up: oft have I --ed up dead men from their graves, Tit. V, 1, 135.
4) to lower by digging: who --s hills because they do aspire, Per. I, 4, 5.
